content.full_text:
A 80-160

August 6, 1980

Mr. Jim Orr
16265 Mt. Baden Powell
Fountain Valley, California 92708

Dear Mr. Orr:

This letter will confirm the advice I provided during our
telephone conversation of today. In your letter of July 9, 1980,
you explained that you anticipate selling various items, including
household appliances/furnishings and personal care products, for
which a portion of your profits will be used for political purposes.
Some of your customers will be advised of your intentions with
respect to the disbursement of a portion of your profits, and pre-
sumably this will provide them with an incentive of make purchases.
However, these customers will receive no monetary incentives since
they will pay your normal price for the goods or services provided.

You asked whether or not the funds received from your customers
will be contributions under the provisions of the Political Reform
Act.

Based on the facts you provided, I advised you that funds
received from your customers will not be contributions because
your customers will be receiving full and adequate consideration
for their money. In addition, I advised you that you will incur
reporting obligations under the Act as an "expenditure committee"
when you have made aggregate independent expenditures of $500 or
more in a calendar year.

If you have additional questions, please contact me.

Yours truly,

John C. Greenwood
Consultant

sections.question: You asked whether or not the funds received from your customers will be contributions under the provisions of the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion: Based on the facts you provided, I advised you that funds received from your customers will not be contributions because your customers will be receiving full and adequate consideration for their money. In addition, I advised you that you will incur reporting obligations under the Act as an "expenditure committee" when you have made aggregate independent expenditures of $500 or more in a calendar year.
sections.question_synthetic: Are funds received from customers who purchase goods with knowledge that profits will be used for political purposes considered contributions under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Funds from customers are not contributions because customers receive full and adequate consideration through normal market-price purchases. The requestor will have reporting obligations as an expenditure committee once aggregate independent expenditures reach $500 or more in a calendar year.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard advice letter format with clear question and conclusion sections. Telephone conversation confirmation letter dated August 6, 1980. Response by John C. Greenwood, Consultant.

In addition, I advised you that you will incur reporting obligations under the Act as an "expenditure committee" when you have made aggregate independent expenditures of $500 or more in a calendar year.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that funds raised through selling goods at normal prices, with customers aware that profits will support political purposes, do not constitute contributions under the Political Reform Act, but establishes expenditure committee reporting obligations.
